justification: O(n) two-Set algorithm used (not nested loops). AC4 correctly returns each duplicate exactly once via Set semantics. Tests explicitly cover AC4 edge case (3+ occurrences returned once). Notes explain why O(n) was chosen and why O(n²) would fail. AC3 performance test included with justified threshold adjustment. All 5 ACs mapped in acceptance_criteria_coverage. The only minor gap: confidence section notes CI threshold tuning but doesn't explicitly state AC3 'requires runtime validation, not a unit test' — however it does include a performance test and discusses the distinction between Jest overhead and production runtime, which functionally addresses the same concern.

observations.text: Two-Set O(n) algorithm is elegant and correct — avoids unnecessary Map counting overhead
AC4 edge case explicitly tested: 'each duplicate ID appears exactly once even when it occurs 3+ times'
All 5 ACs mapped with status, test name, and notes in acceptance_criteria_coverage
Deviation documented for 200ms test threshold with clear justification (Jest overhead vs bare-Node runtime)
8 tests total covering all ACs plus edge cases (single element, all-identical, multiple distinct duplicates)

Confidence section doesn't explicitly note that AC3 is a runtime characteristic that cannot be fully verified by a unit test — it includes a performance test but frames concerns around CI threshold tuning rather than the fundamental limitation
